did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes FDS-Net, a deep learning network for skin cancer segmentation. It utilizes a wavelet-based semantic information decoupling module, a frequency and spatial domain feature fusion module, and a Mahalanobis distance-based progressive decoder. The model was trained and evaluated on public dermoscopic datasets (ISIC2018, etc.) to segment skin lesions, achieving 82.42% mIOU and 89.51% mDSC.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents FDS-Net, a novel deep learning architecture for skin cancer segmentation. It details the design of specific modules (wavelet-based semantic information decoupling, feature fusion, Mahalanobis distance-based decoder), describes experimental setups on public datasets (ISIC2016, ISIC2017, ISIC2018), and reports original quantitative results (82.42% mIOU, 89.51% mDSC) and ablation studies. This constitutes an original technical study producing new results.

title: FDS-Net: Frequency-Domain-Driven Multi-Scale Supervised Contrastive Learning Network for Skin Cancer Segmentation
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenges of low visual contrast and surface artifacts in automated skin lesion segmentation by proposing FDS-Net, a frequency-domain-driven multi-scale supervised contrastive learning network. The architecture incorporates a wavelet-based semantic information decoupling module to separate lesion, normal, and boundary regions, alongside a dual-branch feature fusion module to capture complementary frequency and spatial domain information. To refine segmentation of blurred boundaries, the model utilizes a Mahalanobis distance-based progressive decoder that dynamically guides feature reconstruction. The method was evaluated on three public dermoscopic datasets from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC2016, ISIC2017, and ISIC2018). Experimental results demonstrated that FDS-Net achieved state-of-the-art performance, attaining 82.42% mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) and 89.51% mean Dice Similarity Coefficient (mDSC) on the ISIC2018 dataset. These findings substantiate the model's superiority and universality in handling complex dermatological images compared to existing deep learning approaches.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion masks (lesion, normal, and boundary regions)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes FDS-Net for 'skin cancer segmentation' and 'automated skin lesion segmentation'. The model predicts pixel-wise masks for lesion, normal, and boundary transition regions. Performance is evaluated using segmentation metrics: mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) and mean Dice Similarity Coefficient (mDSC) on ISIC2016, ISIC2017, and ISIC2018 datasets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on quantitative metrics (mIoU, mDSC) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC2018.notes: FDS-Net surpassed the second-best method, ConDSeg, by 1.44% in mIoU and 1.05% in mDSC. The paper states FDS-Net achieved the highest performance in terms of mDSC and mIoU across ISIC-2016, ISIC-2017, and ISIC-2018 datasets, significantly outperforming existing approaches including U-Net, U-Net++, Attention U-Net, CE-Net, TransFuse, MSRF-Net, MALUNet, DCSAU-Net, LightM-UNet, U-KAN, VM-UNet, AC-MambaSeg, and ConDSeg.

ISIC2016.notes: The paper reports that FDS-Net achieved the highest performance in terms of mDSC and mIoU on the ISIC-2016 dataset compared to state-of-the-art methods, but specific numerical values for this dataset are not provided in the text chunks.

ISIC2017.notes: The paper reports that FDS-Net achieved the highest performance in terms of mDSC and mIoU on the ISIC-2017 dataset compared to state-of-the-art methods, but specific numerical values for this dataset are not provided in the text chunks. Ablation studies were conducted on this dataset.

Ablation Study (ISIC2017).notes: Specific numerical values for ablation components are not provided in the text chunks, but the paper notes that WSD significantly improves segmentation performance and that integrating frequency-domain information with spatial features (FDFA) leads to a substantial improvement in mIoU and mDSC. Adding MBPD markedly improves boundary delineation.

Backbone Architecture Comparison (ISIC20.notes: Res2Net-50 slightly outperformed PVT in both mIoU and mDSC. Deeper networks (ResNet-50, PVT) achieved higher performance than shallower ones (ResNet-18, ResNet-34). Specific numerical values are not provided in the text chunks.

Batch Size Impact (ISIC2017 implied by c.notes: Model performance gradually improved with increasing batch size. Specific numerical values are not provided in the text chunks.

Decoder Distance Metric Comparison (ISIC.notes: Mahalanobis distance was found to be more comprehensive and effective than Euclidean distance. Specific numerical values are not provided in the text chunks.

Covariance Matrix Calculation Methods (I.notes: The learnable multivariate variance diagonal covariance matrix provided the best balance between performance and efficiency. Specific numerical values are not provided in the text chunks.

Window Size Impact on Decoder (ISIC2017.notes: Specific numerical values are not provided in the text chunks.

architecture_used.main_model: FDS-Net (Frequency-Domain-Driven Multi-Scale Supervised Contrastive Learning Network)
architecture_used.pretraining: Res2Net-50 encoder backbone initialized with pre-trained weights

modules: Wavelet-based Semantic Information Decoupling (WSD) module: Separates image frequency-domain information into lesion, normal, and boundary regions using wavelet transform.

Frequency and Spatial Domain Feature Fusion (FDFA) module: Dual-branch parallel architecture. Left branch uses layer normalization, 1x1 convolution for spatial attention, global average pooling, and two-layer linear transformation for channel weights. Right branch transforms input to frequency domain via Fourier transform, applies astroid mask for low/high-frequency decomposition, processes low-frequency via 3x3 standard and dilated convolutions, and combines with high-frequency features.

Mahalanobis Distance-based Progressive Decoder (MBPD): Uses diagonal covariance matrix to compute Mahalanobis distance between features in adaptive windows. Utilizes learnable multivariate variance diagonal covariance matrix for feature correlation modeling.
architecture_details.loss_function: Comprehensive loss system incorporating supervised contrastive learning and semantic prior constraints
architecture_details.training_schedule: Early stopping mechanism and learning rate scheduler employed

classification_or_output_head.details: Produces pixel-wise masks for lesion, normal, and boundary transition regions. No specific classifier head name reported, but utilizes a progressive decoder for feature reconstruction.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation including geometric transformations, color adjustments, occlusion, and elastic deformations

conclusions: The proposed FDS-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance in skin lesion segmentation, attaining 82.42% mIoU and 89.51% mDSC on the ISIC2018 dataset, surpassing the second-best method (ConDSeg) by 1.44% in mIoU and 1.05% in mDSC.

FDS-Net demonstrates superior robustness and universality across three public dermoscopic datasets (ISIC2016, ISIC2017, and ISIC2018), significantly outperforming existing deep learning approaches including U-Net, U-Net++, Attention U-Net, CE-Net, TransFuse, MSRF-Net, MALUNet, DCSAU-Net, LightM-UNet, U-KAN, VM-UNet, AC-MambaSeg, and ConDSeg.

The integration of frequency-domain information with spatial-domain features via the Frequency and Spatial Domain Feature Fusion (FDFA) module leads to substantial improvements in mIoU and mDSC, enabling better capture of multi-scale lesion patterns.

The Wavelet-based Semantic Information Decoupling (WSD) module significantly improves segmentation performance by decoupling frequency-domain information into lesion, normal, and boundary regions, which helps distinguish lesion semantics from surrounding tissue.
The Mahalanobis Distance-based Progressive Decoder (MBPD) markedly improves boundary delineation and handles ambiguous boundaries more effectively than Euclidean distance metrics.

Among tested backbone architectures, Res2Net-50 slightly outperformed Pyramid Vision Transformer (PVT) in both mIoU and mDSC, highlighting the importance of strong feature extraction capabilities for dermatological image segmentation.
The model exhibits robustness in avoiding false negatives, successfully detecting lesions even in cases with atypical morphology and texture that are underrepresented in training data.

The study acknowledges limitations related to the two-dimensional nature of dermoscopy and the overlap of surface interferents (such as hair and textures) with small, early-stage lesions, which can lead to information aliasing and misclassification by models.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on public dermoscopic datasets (ISIC2016, ISIC2017, ISIC2018) and lacks external validation on independent clinical datasets or multi-center data, limiting generalizability assessment.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance superiority is claimed based solely on point estimates of mIoU and mDSC.

The Mahalanobis Distance-based Progressive Decoder uses a diagonal covariance matrix, which the authors acknowledge may face limitations in modeling complex inter-feature dependencies in multi-modal scenarios.

The model's performance is constrained by the inherent limitations of 2D dermoscopy, specifically information aliasing caused by surface artifacts (hair, textures) overlapping with small, early-stage lesions.
The study acknowledges a scarcity of high-quality medical images for rare skin diseases, suggesting potential bias or poor performance on underrepresented lesion types.
Specific numerical results for ISIC2016 and ISIC2017 are not provided in the text, preventing direct quantitative comparison for those datasets despite claims of state-of-the-art performance.
Ablation studies on decoder window sizes and covariance matrix calculations were conducted, but specific numerical values for these ablations are not reported in the provided text chunks.
